_meta._file_role: v15 retrofit fragment for spacetime-symmetry-groups. SUPERSEDES MAP_v14_entry_spacetime_symmetries.json. Changes: (1) cell_refs added to all 3 outgoing uses-classification edges (lightly-forced v14.4 finding); (2) 1 new cross-classification edge to ade-lie-algebras with subtype `derives-from` (the real-forms relation); (3) related_entries updated: ade-lie-algebras removed (now typed edge); ade-modular-invariants removed (incidental thematic only); sm-rep-content retained (genuine cross-reference, not naturally a cross-classification subtype).
_meta._notes: Convention for cell_refs on cross-classification edges (not specified in v15 spec §5.1): cell_refs pins source-side cells, paralleling bears-on. Documented in findings as open question for v15.2 spec clarification.

nodes.label: Spacetime symmetry groups
nodes.full_name: Classification of spacetime symmetry groups used in physical theories
nodes.description: The classification of symmetry groups that act on spacetime in physical theories: the Galilean group of Newtonian spacetime, the Poincaré group of Minkowski spacetime, the conformal extension of Poincaré (or its 2D infinite-dimensional analog Virasoro), the (anti-)de Sitter groups of curved-spacetime cosmological backgrounds, and the diffeomorphism group of general relativity. Each is a Lie group (finite-dimensional in most cases; the 2D conformal case is the famous infinite-dimensional exception) whose representations classify particle content and field content in theories invariant under it. Wigner's 1939 classification of the unitary irreducible representations of the Poincaré group — by mass-squared (a Casimir) and spin/helicity — is the single most consequential application: it defines what a 'particle' is in relativistic quantum field theory.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Symmetry groups acting globally on a spacetime manifold (or locally, in the case of Diff), used as the symmetry group of a physical theory's action. Excludes: internal gauge groups (classified separately in the SM rep content entry); discrete spacetime symmetries (P, T, C); supersymmetry extensions.
nodes.internal_structure: The major spacetime symmetry groups are organized by which of the following operations they include: spatial rotations (always present); spatial translations (always present); time translations (always present); spatial boosts (Galilean: instantaneous; Lorentz: relativistic); spatial dilations (conformal only); special conformal transformations (conformal only); cosmological-constant-induced curvature (dS/AdS only); arbitrary smooth diffeomorphisms (Diff only). Most of the simple components of these groups are real forms of complex simple Lie algebras of ADE type — see the outgoing cross-classification edge to ade-lie-algebras.

classification_axes.name: symmetry-type
classification_axes.description: The six major spacetime symmetry families. Each label denotes a FAMILY of groups parameterized by spacetime dimension; cell content names the specific group and notes dimensional dependence.

cells.content: Gal(d) = SO(d-1) ⋉ (translations × boosts) ⋉ time-translation; non-relativistic; instantaneous boosts; central extensions (Bargmann) carry physical mass parameter
realized_examples: non-relativistic quantum mechanics (with Bargmann central extension)
non-relativistic field theories (cold atoms, condensed matter at low temperature)
cells.description: Galilean group: symmetry group of Newtonian spacetime. The central extension (Bargmann 1954) is required for projective representations in QM.

cells.content: ISO(d-1, 1) = SO(d-1, 1) ⋉ ℝ^d; Lorentz group SO(d-1, 1) ≃ Spin(d-1, 1)/Z_2; unitary irreps classified by Wigner 1939
realized_examples: the Standard Model (Poincaré-invariant at energies below the Planck scale)
all of relativistic QFT in flat spacetime
cells.description: Poincaré group: symmetry group of Minkowski spacetime. Wigner's 1939 classification of unitary irreps by (mass², spin/helicity) defines the notion of 'particle' in relativistic QFT.

cells.content: For d ≥ 3: SO(d, 2) ≃ Conf(d-1, 1); finite-dimensional. For d = 2: infinite-dimensional Virasoro × Virasoro with central charge c
realized_examples: conformal field theories at critical points
cells.description: Conformal group: extension of Poincaré by spatial dilations and special conformal transformations. The d = 2 case is qualitatively different — infinite-dimensional Virasoro algebra.

cells.content: SO(d, 1) acting on d-dimensional de Sitter spacetime (positive cosmological constant); finite-dimensional; no positive-energy unitary irreps
realized_examples: our observed accelerating universe (approximately dS in the far future under ΛCDM)
cells.description: de Sitter group: isometry group of de Sitter spacetime. The absence of a globally-defined positive-energy generator has major consequences for the swampland conjectures and dS thermodynamics (Gibbons-Hawking).

cells.content: SO(d-1, 2) acting on d-dimensional anti-de Sitter spacetime (negative cosmological constant); positive-energy unitary irreps exist; isomorphic to Conf(d-2, 1) (the AdS/CFT isomorphism at the group level)
realized_examples: AdS_5 × S^5 in Type IIB string theory
exact non-perturbative QFT-like theories with negative cc
cells.description: Anti-de Sitter group. The group-level isomorphism SO(d-1,2) ≃ Conf(d-2,1) is the structural origin of AdS/CFT.

cells.content: Diff(M): the full diffeomorphism group of a smooth manifold M; infinite-dimensional; the gauge group of general relativity
cells.description: General-coordinate (diffeomorphism) group. Infinite-dimensional; gauge of GR. Source of the 'problem of time' in canonical quantum gravity.

closure_status.description: Complete as a classification of the major spacetime symmetry families. Each family's mathematical structure is fully worked out. Does not extend to: (i) which family holds at the Planck scale (QG frontier); (ii) supersymmetric extensions; (iii) whether spacetime symmetry is fundamental or emergent.

predictive_yield.prediction: Existence and classification of particle types in relativistic QFT: each particle is a unitary irrep of Poincaré, labeled by (mass, spin) or (zero mass, helicity). Higher-spin massless particles (s > 2 in 4D) excluded in interacting theories.
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Every observed elementary particle in the SM fits Wigner's classification.

predictive_yield.prediction: Galilean-invariant non-relativistic QM admits projective representations labeled by mass (Bargmann central extension); mass is a conserved superselection sector in NRQM.
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: The mass superselection rule is a routine feature of NRQM.

predictive_yield.prediction: 2D conformal field theories at criticality have infinite-dimensional Virasoro symmetry; c < 1 minimal models classified by (p, p') labels (ADE / minimal-model classification).
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Belavin, Polyakov, Zamolodchikov 1984, Nucl. Phys. B 241, 333
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: 2D Ising criticality, tricritical Ising, three-state Potts — matched to minimal-model predictions to high precision

citations: Belavin, Polyakov, Zamolodchikov 1984, Nucl. Phys. B 241, 333
Weinberg 1995, The Quantum Theory of Fields Vol. I, Cambridge

edges.description: The QG frontier sits structurally between Poincaré (the symmetry of low-energy flat-spacetime physics) and Diff (the gauge group of GR). The QG frontier asks which spacetime symmetry holds at the Planck scale and how the two reconcile.

The cosmological-constant frontier is structurally the question of which constant-curvature spacetime nature realizes: dS, AdS, or Minkowski. The symmetry-groups classification names the three but does not predict which.
AdS/CFT depends on the group-level isomorphism SO(d-1,2) ≃ Conf(d-2,1). The bears-on is structural-only: the classification names the isomorphism but does not solve the paradox.

QFT (relativistic) is built on the Poincaré-invariance axiom; unitary irreps of Poincaré (Wigner 1939) classify the asymptotic particle content. Conformal field theory (cell-stsym-conformal) is a sub-domain of QFT using the conformal symmetry-type. v15 retrofit: cell_refs now structurally pin the relevant cells.
citations: Streater, Wightman 1964, PCT, Spin and Statistics, and All That, Benjamin

edges.description: GR uses the general-coordinate (Diff) symmetry as its gauge group; the Einstein-Hilbert action is diffeomorphism-invariant. v15 retrofit: cell_refs pins the Diff cell.
citations: Wald 1984, General Relativity, University of Chicago Press

edges.description: Stat-mech uses Galilean invariance for non-relativistic systems and conformal invariance at critical points (BPZ 1984 and successors). v15 retrofit: cell_refs pin both cells.
citations: Cardy 1996, Scaling and Renormalization in Statistical Physics, Cambridge

edges.description: The simple components of the finite-dimensional spacetime symmetry groups are real forms of complex simple Lie algebras of ADE type. Lorentz so(3,1) ≃ sl(2,ℂ)_ℝ is a real form of A_1 × A_1; conformal so(2,4) is a real form of A_3 = D_3 = sl(4)/so(6); de Sitter so(4,1) and anti-de Sitter so(3,2) are real forms of D-type complex algebras. The relation does NOT apply to the Galilean cell (different algebraic structure), the 2D-conformal sub-case (Virasoro is infinite-dimensional, not ADE), or the Diff cell (also infinite-dimensional). cell_refs pins the source-side cells where the relation applies; this convention (cell_refs as source-side pins on cross-classification edges) is documented in v15.1 findings as an open spec clarification for v15.2.
citations: Helgason 2001, Differential Geometry, Lie Groups, and Symmetric Spaces (AMS) — real forms of complex Lie algebras
Hsiang 1975, Cohomology Theory of Topological Transformation Groups (Springer) — real-form structure for spacetime groups
